text: Alan Kay as a formative member of the amazing Xerox Palo Alto Research Center has a great place in history, as they created most of what the computer industry took the next 50 years to commercialize. But his statement that "it is easier to invent the future than predict it", is completely untrue. Bret Victor's work showed prototypes of a future programming interface that is proving extremely difficult to invent. I have little doubt that future programming interfaces will be much more "live" and interactive. Fairly easy to mock-up isolated examples, but extremely difficult to build in practice on larger real projects. Another great counter-example are the written works of Jules Verne. He predicted the future so accurately, and so far in advance, that his novels were not accepted by his publisher as fiction, but were considered wild fantasies and therefore only suitable for children. He predicted ecoterrorism, radar, television, rays of unusual qualities, aerial combat, and probably 100 more things that actually happened, that were many decades ahead of their invention.

text: creating dynamic form layouts in Beads

elements.text: creating dynamic form layouts in Beads

text: creating dynamic form layouts in Beads

elements.text: creating dynamic form layouts in Beads

text: It is fun to consider the future of computing, but people in the trenches who are trying to do basic automation need the basics handled as well, so i made an example in Beads showing the dynamic nature of layouts, where you can make a single form that adapts to whatever screen layout, orientation, and pixel density it encounters, so a single code base can run on desktop, tablet, and mobile. Mobile forms are tricky because the label moves above the field due to the narrowness. Anyway if people are interested in taking Beads for a spin, it is downloadable at <http://www.beadslang.com/downloads/beads.zip>. See the README file at the to level for installation instructions.

text: I like the idea of the need to "abolish the ceremony". I know you talk about ceremony about programming/managing data, but one can extend it to other kind of ceremony around acts of programming. Which sometime is not essentially necessary but is the result of the culture. Like the use of commands in terminals with dark background and flashy characters and no margin. I see programmers that need this to feel good even if it can be seen as terrible in some UI/UX sense. And young people see this as the ritual to become a real programmer.

text: With your third article I discovered the category approach in linguistics. And it is interesting to draw a parallel with programming. At least knowing that our mind is a kind of "catorygizer" is helpful in designing tools for thoughts and especially for building those tools with programming.

text:
And if you read French, I recommand this book by *Gilles Dowek* (a french computer scientist and philosopher) : *"Ce dont on ne peut parler, il faut l’écrire"*. Let me try to translate the title: "what you can't tell by speaking you have to tell by writing" (hope it is close enough to te Dowek idea!).

It is a very interesting book wich compares natural languages to other languages. In fact in French we have two separate words: "langues" are natural languages (English, French...) and "languages" are all other languages (math notations, music scores, programming languages...).

Natural languages were first spoken before written, and others were create to write stuff that benefit in expressivness when written. In fact writing was "invented" not to write down what humans were talking about, but to count things, it was a very utilitarian use. It helped us a lot in the action of counting and doing business.

I found very inspiring the separation of the two kinds of languages, and what is different and similar.

text:
From feedback here and through other channels I can already tell that I focused too much on the linguistics aspect of it. As you will hopefully see soon in the coming articles, I'm much more interested in the cognition part of it, so I should probably talk about cognitive science instead of linguistics to prevent people to come to the wrong conclusions before I even get started with the interesting bits.

In other words, if you think I'll be talking about generative linguistics and all the well-understood parallels to information theory and computer science, there's a major plot twist incoming. :wink:

text: <https://blog.cerebralab.com/Neural_networks_as_non-leaky_mathematical_abstraction>
<https://blog.cerebralab.com/Neural_networks_as_non-leaky_mathematical_abstraction>

> It’s important because it’s a way to conceptualize why neural networks are in a way better than classical ML algorithms. Because this lack of leaks means that anyone can play around with them without breaking the whole thing and being thrown one level down. Want to change the shape ? Sure. Want to change the activation function ? Sure. Want to add a state function to certain elements ? Go ahead. Want to add random connections between various elements ? Don’t see why not… etc.


> I truly think that they are among the first of a "new" type of mathematical abstractions. They allow people that don't have the dozen+ years background of learning applied mathematics, to do applied mathematics.

text: interesting article! I think I disagree with the author's point that mathematical abstractions are more leaky than CS ones. traditional "rigorous" math education (I'm thinking bachelors thru research level) encourages folks to understand the entire stack of abstractions, but that's not terribly different from a CS student learning about transistors and basic circuit design

text: there are many fields in math in which nearly no one understands every piece like this. e.g. you can use the "big machines" of algebraic topology without understanding all the proofs behind them (and in many cases, if you were not primarily a topologist, fully understanding would be a waste of your time). known theorems are the "API". and like many APIs, you need to well understand domain concepts (terms used in the statement of the theorem) in order to use them correctly

text: Hi guys! My name is Shaily. I'm a software engineer in web development, although relatively new to the field. I recently started working for a startup where we're building a goal-oriented wealth management platform, essentially for millennials. Any suggestions/questions are welcome. I'm super excited to know and share more interesting stuff! :slightly_smiling_face:

text: Thanks <@UBKNXPBAB> :slightly_smiling_face: the term API is more describing the theme than the mechanics. My envisioned purpose is to make it so you can take someone else’s library/API and quickly kill off generic code and unused paths.

text: ... You're calling this 2020/01 Week 1. But if you're publishing on Monday, that's December 30th. I think it'd make sense to date the newsletter based on the day of its release — so this would be 2019/12 Week 5. Yes? No?

text:
>  How does spectrum do for permalinks?
They have permalinks for threads and also messages within a thread. BTW, I happened to notice some recent FUD around spectrum on their self discussion chat: <https://spectrum.chat/spectrum/general/is-spectrum-dead~ae821b96-0005-4d1d-bd74-9ec0802f3a8d>

attachments.text: Sorry if the title is a bit clickbaity; nonetheless I think this is a legit question given the project's contribution…

text: Wow. It does look like Spectrum's devs are doing something different now — <https://github.com/withspectrum/spectrum/graphs/contributors>. Not sure if that means the project is dead, or if they're just changing how they work on it, but it's probably worth being cautious about hitching our wagon to that star (is that the expression?)

text:
<@U8A5MS6R1> I've been in a few discourse communities, like <https://clojureverse.org>, and I think overall that I like it as a platform. Off the top of my head, I have one big frustration: lazy-loading of comments when reading long threads, which breaks the browser's find-on-page — a clash between the SPA culture that birthed Discourse and the static HTML culture birthed web forums. I also found the lack of comment threading to be a mixed bag, but that's not a gripe with Discourse specifically, and the support for quote-replying to sections of prior comments helped alleviate that (though the `[quote="user, post:2, topic:5229"]` syntax is kinda nasty). Ultimately, though, I don't know if it's a good idea to move the community from chat to a forum. That'd really change the feel of participating, I think — and _I think_ I prefer the rapidity and lightness of chat, though I'm not sure. I want to know what you think — are you especially fond of Discourse, and if so why? Or is it just another of the more notable options that we should weigh against the others?

Other folks in this thread — stick with a chat service, or move to a forum?

text:
I'm neutral on Discourse. I'm not even an active user on any Discourse forum. I just wanted to put it out there while this topic is hot, because it has come up before and in terms of web forums it seems like one of the nicer ones.

>  Other folks in this thread — stick with a chat service, or move to a forum?
This is the key question I think. Or have both and split the community. I do think there are folks that don't post or follow the chat that much because of the ephemeral nature. Maybe even a mailing list would suffice. Now that we have the tinyletter, if a forum/mailing list is spun up - it could provide at least one integration point - we would post the weekly summary to the forum/ml also.

text: <@ULM3U6275> which recording tool do you favor for screen captures? The mac comes with Quictime screen recording, but those files do get rather large. Be nice to find something that compresses as it goes.
